title: Why Energy Matters
paragraphs: Climate change is the most consequential reason energy matters. Burning fossil fuels for electricity, heat, and transport accounts for roughly 75% of global greenhouse gas emissions. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change has made clear that limiting global warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels requires halving global emissions by 2030 and reaching net zero by mid-century. This is fundamentally an energy problem. Decarbonising electricity generation is the first and most tractable step — solar and wind are already cheaper than new fossil generation in most markets. But electricity is only part of the picture. Decarbonising transport, industry, buildings, and agriculture requires a far broader transformation: electrification where possible, green hydrogen where electrification is impractical, efficiency improvements everywhere, and potentially carbon capture for residual emissions that cannot be eliminated.

Energy security — the reliable availability of energy at affordable prices — has shaped geopolitics for over a century. The 1973 OPEC oil embargo demonstrated how energy dependence could be weaponised, triggering recessions across the Western world. Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 exposed Europe's dangerous dependence on Russian natural gas, causing energy prices to spike and accelerating the continent's pivot toward renewables and LNG diversification. Energy-exporting nations — Saudi Arabia, Russia, Qatar, Australia, the United States — wield outsized geopolitical influence precisely because of their resource endowments. The energy transition is reshaping these power dynamics: countries with abundant sunshine, wind, and critical minerals (lithium, cobalt, rare earth elements) are emerging as the new energy superpowers, while petrostates face the prospect of stranded assets — fossil fuel reserves that may never be economically extractable.

The relationship between energy consumption and economic development is one of the strongest correlations in economics. No country has industrialised without dramatically increasing its energy use. Access to cheap, reliable energy enables manufacturing, agriculture, transportation, communication, healthcare, and education. The Human Development Index correlates closely with per-capita energy consumption up to a threshold of roughly 100 gigajoules per person per year, after which additional energy use yields diminishing returns in human welfare. This creates a profound tension at the heart of the energy transition: developed nations need to reduce their energy-related emissions rapidly, while developing nations need to increase their energy access to achieve basic living standards. Resolving this tension equitably is one of the defining challenges of international climate policy.

Energy poverty remains a stark global reality. Approximately 750 million people — nearly one in ten humans — lack access to electricity. Around 2.3 billion people still rely on traditional biomass (wood, charcoal, dung) for cooking, causing indoor air pollution that kills an estimated 3.2 million people annually. Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and parts of Southeast Asia bear the brunt of this deficit. The falling cost of solar PV and battery storage is creating new pathways to energy access — off-grid solar home systems and mini-grids can reach communities faster and cheaper than extending centralised grid infrastructure. But the scale of the challenge remains enormous, and progress has been uneven.

The sheer scale of global energy consumption is difficult to comprehend. Humanity consumes approximately 580 exajoules of primary energy per year — equivalent to the energy released by roughly 14 billion tonnes of oil, or about 13,800 Hiroshima-sized nuclear weapons detonated every single day. Global electricity generation alone exceeds 29,000 terawatt-hours annually. Meeting growing demand while simultaneously decarbonising supply requires building new clean energy capacity at an unprecedented rate. The International Energy Agency estimates that annual clean energy investment needs to roughly triple from current levels — from approximately USD $1.8 trillion per year to over $4 trillion per year by 2030 — to stay on a net-zero pathway. This is not merely an engineering challenge; it is a financial, political, and institutional challenge of the first order.

Energy intersects with virtually every other domain of human concern. National security depends on energy supply chains. Public health depends on clean air and access to modern energy services. Agriculture depends on energy for irrigation, fertiliser production (the Haber-Bosch process consumes roughly 1-2% of global energy), and cold chains. Digital infrastructure — data centres, telecommunications, computing — is a rapidly growing energy consumer, with data centre electricity use projected to double or triple by 2030 as artificial intelligence workloads expand. Water treatment and desalination are energy-intensive. Transport systems are in the early stages of a wholesale shift from internal combustion to electric powertrains. Understanding energy is not a specialist concern — it is a prerequisite for understanding the modern world.
keyPoints: Roughly 75% of global greenhouse gas emissions come from energy production and use, making decarbonisation of the energy system the central climate challenge.

Energy security has shaped geopolitics for over a century — from the 1973 oil embargo to Russia's gas leverage over Europe — and the transition is creating new strategic dependencies on critical minerals.
No country has industrialised without dramatically increasing energy consumption; per-capita energy use correlates strongly with human development up to a threshold.
Approximately 750 million people lack electricity access, and 2.3 billion still cook with traditional biomass, causing 3.2 million premature deaths annually from indoor air pollution.
Global primary energy consumption is roughly 580 exajoules per year, and clean energy investment needs to roughly triple to over USD $4 trillion per year by 2030 for a net-zero pathway.
Energy costs and availability directly determine the viability of food production, manufacturing, digital infrastructure, water treatment, and healthcare systems worldwide.
The energy transition creates a fundamental equity tension: developed nations must cut emissions while developing nations must increase energy access to achieve basic living standards.
Data centre electricity consumption is projected to double or triple by 2030 as AI workloads grow, adding a significant new category of energy demand.
